docs.title: handbook
docs.text:
This is where I gather useful knowledge.

Main area of interest:

  • Linear Algebra
  • Machine Learning
  • Statistics
  • Probability
  • Coding practices

docs.title: Understanding Transformers and the Attention Mechanism
Introduction
docs.text: Transformers have revolutionized natural language processing and many other domains since their introduction in the "Attention Is All You Need" paper (Vaswhatever et al., 2017). This chapter breaks down their core mechanisms and explains how they process information.

docs.title: Core Architecture
docs.text:
The Transformer architecture consists of two main components: 1. An encoder that processes the input sequence 2. A decoder that generates the output sequence

Both components are built from stacks of identical layers, each containing: - Multi-head self-attention mechanisms - Feed-forward neural networks - Layer normalization and residual connections

docs.title: The Attention Mechanism: Step by Step
1. Input Embedding and Positional Encoding
docs.text:
• First, input tokens are converted to embeddings (dense vectors)
  • Positional encodings are added to maintain sequence order information
  • These use sine and cosine functions of different frequencies: pos_encoding(pos, 2i) = sin(pos/10000^(2i/d_model)) pos_encoding(pos, 2i+1) = cos(pos/10000^(2i/d_model))

docs.title: 2. Query, Key, and Value Computation
docs.text:
The attention mechanism uses three main components: - Query (Q): What we're looking for - Key (K): What we match against - Value (V): What we want to retrieve

These are computed by multiplying the input embeddings by learned weight matrices: 

Q = input × W_Q
K = input × W_K
V = input × W_V

docs.title: 3. Attention Score Calculation
docs.text:
The attention scores are computed in several steps:

a. Compatibility Function 

scores = (Q × K^T) / √d_k
where d_k is the dimension of the key vectors, and the division by √d_k prevents values from becoming too large for the softmax.

b. Masking (if necessary) In decoder self-attention or for padding tokens: - Set unwanted attention scores to -infinity - This ensures these positions have zero weight after softmax

c. Softmax Application 

attention_weights = softmax(scores)
This normalizes the scores into probabilities that sum to 1.

docs.title: 4. Computing the Output
docs.text:
The final attention output is computed by: 

attention_output = attention_weights × V

docs.title: 5. Multi-Head Attention
docs.text:
Instead of performing attention once, Transformers use multiple attention heads:

  1. Each head has its own Q, K, V projections
  2. Compute attention independently for each head
  3. Concatenate the results
  4. Project back to the model dimension: 
    MultiHead(Q, K, V) = Concat(head_1, ..., head_h) × W_O
where head_i = Attention(Q × W_Q_i, K × W_K_i, V × W_V_i)

docs.title: Feed-Forward Networks and Residual Connections
docs.text:
After attention, each sub-layer contains:

  1. Layer Normalization 

    norm(x) = γ × (x - μ)/σ + β
    where μ and σ are mean and standard deviation, γ and β are learned parameters

  2. Feed-Forward Network 

    FFN(x) = GELU(xW_1 + b_1)W_2 + b_2

where:

GELU is the Gaussian Error Linear Unit activation function W_1 transforms from model dimension to intermediate dimension (typically 4x larger) W_2 transforms back to model dimension b_1 and b_2 are bias terms

Note: While earlier transformer implementations used ReLU (max(0, x)), modern transformers typically use GELU activation functions which provide smoother gradients.

  1. Residual Connection 
    output = LayerNorm(x + Sublayer(x))

docs.title: Practical Example: Computing Self-Attention
docs.text:
Let's walk through a simplified example with 4-dimensional vectors:

# Input embeddings (batch_size=1, seq_len=3, d_model=4)
X = [
    [1, 0, 1, 0],  # First token
    [0, 1, 0, 1],  # Second token
    [1, 1, 0, 0]   # Third token
]

# Project to Q, K, V (simplified weights)
W_Q = W_K = W_V = I  # Identity matrix for simplicity
Q = K = V = X        # In practice, these would be different

# Compute attention scores
scores = Q × K^T / √4
# Results in:
# [[1.0, 0.5, 0.5],
#  [0.5, 1.0, 0.5],
#  [0.5, 0.5, 1.0]]

# Apply softmax
weights = softmax(scores)
# Results in attention weights focusing most on matching positions

# Final output
output = weights × V
# Each position now contains a weighted mix of values

docs.title: Training and Optimization
docs.text:
Transformers are typically trained using: - Adam optimizer with β1 = 0.9, β2 = 0.98, ε = 10^-9 - Learning rate scheduling with warmup - Dropout for regularization - Label smoothing

The loss function varies by task: - Cross-entropy for classification/language modeling - Custom losses for specific applications

docs.title: Common Variations and Improvements
docs.text:
Modern transformers often include: - Relative positional encoding - Sparse attention patterns - Parameter sharing across layers - Efficient attention approximations

These modifications help with: - Longer sequence handling - Computational efficiency - Task-specific requirements

docs.title: References
docs.text: Note: While this technical explanation is accurate, you should verify specific implementation details against primary sources and documentation for your particular use case.

docs.title: Core Statistical Concepts for Data Science Interviews
docs.text: Statistic basic rules:

docs.title: Common Interview Questions
docs.text:
When would you use a t-test vs z-test?

Let me break down the key differences between t-tests and z-tests and explain when to use each one:

How do you handle missing data?

Content for handling missing data...

What's the difference between correlation and causation?

Content for correlation vs causation...

Explain the central limit theorem

Content for central limit theorem...

How do you detect and handle outliers?

Content for detecting and handling outliers...

What's the difference between parametric and non-parametric tests?

Content for parametric vs non-parametric tests...

How do you choose between different types of regression?

Content for choosing between different types of regression...

Explain cross-validation and its importance

Content for cross-validation and its importance...

docs.title: Key Distinctions
docs.text:
1. Population Standard Deviation

    • Z-test: Used when we KNOW the population standard deviation (σ)
    • T-test: Used when we DON'T know the population standard deviation and must estimate it using sample standard deviation (s)

  2. Sample Size

    • Z-test: Generally used for large samples (n > 30)
    • T-test: Better for small samples (n < 30) because it accounts for extra uncertainty in estimating the standard deviation

  3. Distribution

    • Z-test: Assumes data follows a normal distribution
    • T-test: Uses Student's t-distribution, which has heavier tails than normal distribution to account for additional uncertainty

docs.title: Practical Examples
docs.text:
Scenario 1: Quality Control in Large Manufacturing Plant - Testing widget weights - Years of historical data available - Known population standard deviation - Large daily samples → Use Z-test because you know σ and have large samples

Scenario 2: Medical Research Study - Testing new drug effectiveness - Small patient group (n=20) - No known population standard deviation - Need to estimate variance from sample → Use T-test because of small sample size and unknown σ

docs.title: Heavier Tails of T-Distribution
docs.text:
The t-distribution is defined as:

\[ t = rac{Z}{\sqrt{V/n}} \]

where: - Z follows N(0,1) - V follows χ²(n) (chi-square with n degrees of freedom) - Z and V are independent

The probability density function (PDF) of t-distribution with ν degrees of freedom is:

\[ f(t) = rac{\Gamma( rac{ u + 1}{2})}{\sqrt{ u\pi}\Gamma( rac{ u}{2})}(1 + rac{t^2}{ u})^{- rac{ u + 1}{2}} \]

Compare this to the normal distribution PDF:

\[ f(x) = rac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{- rac{x^2}{2}} \]

docs.title: Convergence to Normal Distribution
docs.text:
As n → ∞, we can prove convergence using:

  1. The Central Limit Theorem for V/n: $$ \frac{V/n - 1}{\sqrt{2/n}} \xrightarrow{d} N(0,1) $$

  2. Therefore, as n → ∞: $$ \sqrt{V/n} \xrightarrow{p} 1 $$

  3. Thus: $$ t = \frac{Z}{\sqrt{V/n}} \xrightarrow{d} Z \sim N(0,1) $$

docs.title: Power Analysis
docs.text:
The power function for a z-test: $$ \pi_Z(\mu) = 1 - \Phi(z_{α/2} - \frac{\mu - \mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}) + \Phi(-z_{α/2} - \frac{\mu - \mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}) $$

The power function for a t-test: $$ \pi_T(\mu) = 1 - F_t(t_{α/2,n-1} - \frac{\mu - \mu_0}{s/\sqrt{n}}) + F_t(-t_{α/2,n-1} - \frac{\mu - \mu_0}{s/\sqrt{n}}) $$

where: - Φ is the standard normal CDF - F_t is the t-distribution CDF - z_{α/2} is the normal critical value - t_{α/2,n-1} is the t critical value

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: For a set of numbers {x₁, x₂, ..., xₙ}: \(AM = \bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i\)

docs.title: Properties
docs.text:
1. Minimizes squared deviations: AM minimizes \(\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \mu)^2\)

  2. Linear property: \(\bar{ax + b} = a\bar{x} + b\)

  3. Effect of outliers: Highly sensitive to extreme values

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Default measure of central tendency
  • When data points contribute equally
  • Financial calculations (e.g., average daily returns)
  • Physical measurements with random errors

docs.title: Relationship with Standard Deviation
docs.text: For a dataset {x₁, ..., xₙ}: \(\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2 - (\bar{x})^2\)

docs.title: Other properties
docs.text:
• Median: Robustness to outliers
  • Mode: Use in categorical data
  • Relationship between mean, median, mode in skewed distributions

docs.title: Algebraic Properties:
docs.text: $$ Var(aX + b) = a^2Var(X) $$ $$ Var(X + Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y) + 2Cov(X,Y) $$ For independent X,Y: $$ Var(X + Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y) $$

docs.title: Standard Deviation:
docs.text: \(\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}\) or \(s = \sqrt{s^2}\)

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Most common measure of variability
  • Optimal for normal distributions
  • Input for many statistical procedures
  • Basis for least squares estimation

docs.title: Interquartile Range
docs.text:
\(IQR = Q_3 - Q_1\)

where: - Q₁ is the 25th percentile - Q₃ is the 75th percentile

docs.title: 1. Outlier Detection:
docs.text:
• Lower fence: \(Q_1 - 1.5 × IQR\)
  • Upper fence: \(Q_3 + 1.5 × IQR\)
  • Points beyond fences are potential outliers

docs.title: 2. Relationship to Standard Deviation:
docs.text: For normal distribution: - \(IQR ≈ 1.349\sigma\) - Range ≈ 4σ (for moderate sample sizes)

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Non-parametric analyses
  • Robust statistics
  • Box plots
  • Quick dispersion estimates

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: \(CV = \frac{s}{\bar{x}} \times 100\%\)

docs.title: 1. Scale Independence:
docs.text:
• Unitless measure
  • Allows comparison across different scales

docs.title: 2. Limitations:
docs.text:
• Only meaningful for ratio scales
  • Not suitable when mean ≈ 0
  • Not defined for negative values

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Comparing variability across different units
  • Quality control
  • Investment risk assessment
  • Biological variation studies

docs.title: 1. Relationship to Standard Deviation:
docs.text: For normal distribution: \(MAD ≈ 0.8\sigma\)

docs.title: 2. Robustness:
docs.text:
• Less sensitive to outliers than variance
  • L1 norm vs L2 norm for variance

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Robust statistics
  • Financial risk measures
  • Time series analysis
  • Bio statistics

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: For random vectors X = [X₁, ..., Xₚ]: \(\Sigma_{ij} = Cov(X_i, X_j) = E[(X_i - \mu_i)(X_j - \mu_j)]\)

docs.title: 1. Matrix Properties:
docs.text:
• Symmetric: \(\Sigma_{ij} = \Sigma_{ji}\)
  • Positive semi-definite
  • Diagonal elements are variances

docs.title: 2. Eigendecomposition:
docs.text: \(\Sigma = PDP^T\) where: - D: diagonal matrix of eigenvalues - P: matrix of eigenvectors

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Principal Component Analysis
  • Multivariate analysis
  • Portfolio optimization
  • Machine learning algorithms

docs.title: 1. Robustness Hierarchy (most to least robust):
docs.text:
1. IQR
  2. MAD
  3. Standard Deviation
  4. Range

docs.title: 2. Efficiency under Normality (most to least efficient):
docs.text:
1. Standard Deviation
  2. MAD
  3. IQR
  4. Range

docs.title: 3. Computational Complexity:
docs.text:
• Range: O(n)
  • IQR: O(n log n)
  • MAD: O(n)
  • Variance: O(n)
  • Covariance Matrix: O(n²)

docs.title: 7. Selection Guidelines
docs.text:
Use Standard Deviation when: - Data is approximately normal - Need mathematical tractability - Working with inferential statistics

Use IQR when: - Data has outliers - Distribution is skewed - Need robust measure

Use CV when: - Comparing different scales - Working with strictly positive data - Need relative variation

Use MAD when: - Need robust measure - Working with time series - Dealing with non-normal data

docs.title: Discrete Random Variables
docs.text: For a discrete random variable X: \(E[X] = \sum_{x} x \cdot P(X = x)\)

docs.title: Continuous Random Variables
docs.text:
For a continuous random variable X: \(E[X] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \cdot f(x) dx\)

where f(x) is the probability density function.

docs.title: Alternative Notation
docs.text:
• \(E[X]\)
  • \(\mu\)
  • \(\mu_X\)
  • \(\langle X \rangle\)

docs.title: Linearity of Expectation
docs.text:
1. \(E[aX + b] = aE[X] + b\)
  2. \(E[X + Y] = E[X] + E[Y]\)

docs.title: Proof for Discrete Case:
docs.text:
E[aX + b] = ∑(ax + b)P(X = x)
          = a∑xP(X = x) + b∑P(X = x)
          = aE[X] + b    (since ∑P(X = x) = 1)

docs.title: Proof:
docs.text:
For discrete case: 

E[E[X|Y]] = ∑_y E[X|Y=y]P(Y=y)
          = ∑_y ∑_x xP(X=x|Y=y)P(Y=y)
          = ∑_x x∑_y P(X=x|Y=y)P(Y=y)
          = ∑_x xP(X=x)
          = E[X]

docs.title: Functions of Random Variables
docs.text: For any function g(X): \(E[g(X)] = \begin{cases} \sum_x g(x)P(X=x) & \text{discrete case} \\ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} g(x)f(x)dx & \text{continuous case} \end{cases}\)

docs.title: Expected Value of Product
docs.text:
For any two random variables X and Y: \(E[XY] = E[X]E[Y] + Cov(X,Y)\)

For independent variables: \(E[XY] = E[X]E[Y]\)

docs.title: Sample Mean
docs.text: For observations {X₁, ..., Xₙ}: \(\bar{X} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\)

docs.title: Properties of Sample Mean
docs.text:
1. \(E[\bar{X}] = \mu\) (unbiased)
  2. \(Var(\bar{X}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\)
  3. \(SE(\bar{X}) = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\)

docs.title: Jensen's Inequality
docs.text: For convex function g: \(g(E[X]) \leq E[g(X)]\)

docs.title: Markov's Inequality
docs.text: For non-negative X and a > 0: \(P(X \geq a) \leq \frac{E[X]}{a}\)

docs.title: Chebyshev's Inequality
docs.text: For any k > 0: \(P(|X-\mu| \geq k\sigma) \leq \frac{1}{k^2}\)

docs.title: Monte Carlo Estimation
docs.text: \(E[X] \approx \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\)

docs.title: Error in Estimation
docs.text:
Standard Error = \(\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\)

95% Confidence Interval ≈ \(\bar{X} \pm 1.96\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\)

docs.title: Skewness
docs.text: Skewness measures the asymmetry of a probability distribution: - Left-skewed (Negative): Tail extends to the left, mean < median - Right-skewed (Positive): Tail extends to the right, mean > median - Symmetric: Mean = median, skewness = 0

docs.title: Kurtosis
docs.text: Kurtosis measures the "tailedness" of a probability distribution: - Heavy-tailed (Leptokurtic): Higher kurtosis, more extreme values - Light-tailed (Platykurtic): Lower kurtosis, fewer extreme values - Normal (Mesokurtic): Reference kurtosis = 3 for normal distribution

docs.title: Moments of a Distribution
docs.text: Moments are quantitative measures that describe the shape of a distribution: - First Moment: Mean (Expected Value) - Second Moment: Variance and Standard Deviation - Third Moment: Related to Skewness - Fourth Moment: Related to Kurtosis

docs.title: 2. Common Distribution Expected Values and Variances
Normal Distribution N(μ, σ²)
docs.text:
• Expected Value: \(E[X] = \mu\)
  • Variance: \(Var(X) = \sigma^2\)

docs.title: Exponential Distribution (rate λ)
docs.text:
• Expected Value: \(E[X] = \frac{1}{\lambda}\)
  • Variance: \(Var(X) = \frac{1}{\lambda^2}\)

docs.title: Poisson Distribution (rate λ)
docs.text:
• Expected Value: \(E[X] = \lambda\)
  • Variance: \(Var(X) = \lambda\)

docs.title: Binomial Distribution (n trials, probability p)
docs.text:
• Expected Value: \(E[X] = np\)
  • Variance: \(Var(X) = np(1-p)\)

docs.title: Geometric Distribution (probability p)
docs.text:
• Expected Value: \(E[X] = \frac{1}{p}\)
  • Variance: \(Var(X) = \frac{1-p}{p^2}\)

docs.title: kth Moment
docs.text:
The kth moment about zero (raw moment) is defined as:

\(E[X^k] = \begin{cases} \sum_x x^k P(X=x) & \text{discrete case} \\ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x^k f(x)dx & \text{continuous case} \end{cases}\)

docs.title: Central Moments
docs.text:
The kth central moment (moment about the mean) is defined as:

\(E[(X-\mu)^k] = \begin{cases} \sum_x (x-\mu)^k P(X=x) & \text{discrete case} \\ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (x-\mu)^k f(x)dx & \text{continuous case} \end{cases}\)

docs.title: Important Relations
docs.text:
• First Central Moment: \(E[X-\mu] = 0\)
  • Second Central Moment: \(E[(X-\mu)^2] = Var(X)\)
  • Third Standardized Moment: \(E[(X-\mu)^3]/\sigma^3\) (Skewness)
  • Fourth Standardized Moment: \(E[(X-\mu)^4]/\sigma^4\) (Kurtosis)

docs.title: Properties

docs.text: \(\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}\) or \(s = \sqrt{s^2}\)

docs.text: For random vectors X = [X₁, ..., Xₚ]: \(\Sigma_{ij} = Cov(X_i, X_j) = E[(X_i - \mu_i)(X_j - \mu_j)]\)

docs.title: 7. Selection Guidelines
docs.text:
Use Standard Deviation when: - Data is approximately normal - Need mathematical tractability - Working with inferential statistics

Use IQR when: - Data has outliers - Distribution is skewed - Need robust measure

Use CV when: - Comparing different scales - Working with strictly positive data - Need relative variation

Use MAD when: - Need robust measure - Working with time series - Dealing with non-normal data

docs.title: Skewness
docs.text: Skewness measures the asymmetry of a probability distribution: - Left-skewed (Negative): Tail extends to the left, mean < median - Right-skewed (Positive): Tail extends to the right, mean > median - Symmetric: Mean = median, skewness = 0

docs.title: Moments of a Distribution
docs.text: Moments are quantitative measures that describe the shape of a distribution: - First Moment: Mean (Expected Value) - Second Moment: Variance and Standard Deviation - Third Moment: Related to Skewness - Fourth Moment: Related to Kurtosis

docs.title: 2. Common Distribution Expected Values and Variances
Normal Distribution N(μ, σ²)
docs.text:
• Expected Value: \(E[X] = \mu\)
  • Variance: \(Var(X) = \sigma^2\)

docs.title: Exponential Distribution (rate λ)
docs.text:
• Expected Value: \(E[X] = \frac{1}{\lambda}\)
  • Variance: \(Var(X) = \frac{1}{\lambda^2}\)

docs.title: Poisson Distribution (rate λ)
docs.text:
• Expected Value: \(E[X] = \lambda\)
  • Variance: \(Var(X) = \lambda\)

docs.title: Binomial Distribution (n trials, probability p)
docs.text:
• Expected Value: \(E[X] = np\)
  • Variance: \(Var(X) = np(1-p)\)

docs.title: Geometric Distribution (probability p)
docs.text:
• Expected Value: \(E[X] = \frac{1}{p}\)
  • Variance: \(Var(X) = \frac{1-p}{p^2}\)

docs.title: kth Moment
docs.text:
The kth moment about zero (raw moment) is defined as:

\(E[X^k] = \begin{cases} \sum_x x^k P(X=x) & \text{discrete case} \\ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x^k f(x)dx & \text{continuous case} \end{cases}\)

docs.title: Central Moments
docs.text:
The kth central moment (moment about the mean) is defined as:

\(E[(X-\mu)^k] = \begin{cases} \sum_x (x-\mu)^k P(X=x) & \text{discrete case} \\ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (x-\mu)^k f(x)dx & \text{continuous case} \end{cases}\)

docs.title: Important Relations
docs.text:
• First Central Moment: \(E[X-\mu] = 0\)
  • Second Central Moment: \(E[(X-\mu)^2] = Var(X)\)
  • Third Standardized Moment: \(E[(X-\mu)^3]/\sigma^3\) (Skewness)
  • Fourth Standardized Moment: \(E[(X-\mu)^4]/\sigma^4\) (Kurtosis)

docs.title: Discrete Random Variables
docs.text: For a discrete random variable X: \(E[X] = \sum_{x} x \cdot P(X = x)\)

docs.title: Continuous Random Variables
docs.text:
For a continuous random variable X: \(E[X] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \cdot f(x) dx\)

where f(x) is the probability density function.

docs.title: Alternative Notation
docs.text:
• \(E[X]\)
  • \(\mu\)
  • \(\mu_X\)
  • \(\langle X \rangle\)

docs.title: Linearity of Expectation
docs.text:
1. \(E[aX + b] = aE[X] + b\)
  2. \(E[X + Y] = E[X] + E[Y]\)

docs.title: Proof for Discrete Case:
docs.text:
E[aX + b] = ∑(ax + b)P(X = x)
          = a∑xP(X = x) + b∑P(X = x)
          = aE[X] + b    (since ∑P(X = x) = 1)

docs.title: Proof:
docs.text:
For discrete case: 

E[E[X|Y]] = ∑_y E[X|Y=y]P(Y=y)
          = ∑_y ∑_x xP(X=x|Y=y)P(Y=y)
          = ∑_x x∑_y P(X=x|Y=y)P(Y=y)
          = ∑_x xP(X=x)
          = E[X]

docs.title: Functions of Random Variables
docs.text: For any function g(X): \(E[g(X)] = \begin{cases} \sum_x g(x)P(X=x) & \text{discrete case} \\ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} g(x)f(x)dx & \text{continuous case} \end{cases}\)

docs.title: Expected Value of Product
docs.text:
For any two random variables X and Y: \(E[XY] = E[X]E[Y] + Cov(X,Y)\)

For independent variables: \(E[XY] = E[X]E[Y]\)

docs.title: Sample Mean
docs.text: For observations {X₁, ..., Xₙ}: \(\bar{X} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\)

docs.title: Properties of Sample Mean
docs.text:
1. \(E[\bar{X}] = \mu\) (unbiased)
  2. \(Var(\bar{X}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\)
  3. \(SE(\bar{X}) = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\)

docs.title: Jensen's Inequality
docs.text: For convex function g: \(g(E[X]) \leq E[g(X)]\)

docs.title: Markov's Inequality
docs.text: For non-negative X and a > 0: \(P(X \geq a) \leq \frac{E[X]}{a}\)

docs.title: Chebyshev's Inequality
docs.text: For any k > 0: \(P(|X-\mu| \geq k\sigma) \leq \frac{1}{k^2}\)

docs.title: Monte Carlo Estimation
docs.text: \(E[X] \approx \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\)

docs.title: Error in Estimation
docs.text:
Standard Error = \(\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\)

95% Confidence Interval ≈ \(\bar{X} \pm 1.96\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\)

  • F-distribution
  • Exponential and Gamma

  • Beta distribution

  • Discrete Distributions

  • Bernoulli and Binomial
  • Poisson (rare events)
  • Geometric and Negative Binomial
  • Hypergeometric

docs.title: Continuous distributions

docs.title: Sample Space
docs.text: The sample space (Ω) represents all possible outcomes of a random experiment. For example, when rolling a die, Ω = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6}.

docs.title: Events
docs.text: An event is a subset of the sample space. We typically denote events with capital letters (A, B, etc.).

docs.title: Probability Measure
docs.text: For any event A, the probability P(A) must satisfy: - 0 ≤ P(A) ≤ 1 - P(Ω) = 1 - P(∅) = 0

docs.title: Basic Addition Rule
docs.text: For mutually exclusive events A and B: P(A ∪ B) = P(A) + P(B)

docs.title: General Addition Rule
docs.text: For any two events A and B: P(A ∪ B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A ∩ B)

docs.title: Extension to Multiple Events
docs.text: For three events A, B, and C: P(A ∪ B ∪ C) = P(A) + P(B) + P(C) - P(A ∩ B) - P(A ∩ C) - P(B ∩ C) + P(A ∩ B ∩ C)

docs.title: Independent Events
docs.text: Two events A and B are independent if: P(A ∩ B) = P(A) × P(B)

docs.title: Dependent Events
docs.text: For dependent events, use conditional probability: P(A ∩ B) = P(A) × P(B|A)

docs.title: Chain Rule
docs.text: For multiple events: P(A ∩ B ∩ C) = P(A) × P(B|A) × P(C|A ∩ B)

docs.title: Mutually Exclusive Events
docs.text: Events A and B are mutually exclusive if: - A ∩ B = ∅ - P(A ∩ B) = 0

docs.title: Complement Rule
docs.text: For any event A: P(A') = 1 - P(A)

docs.title: Conditional Probability
docs.text: P(B|A) = P(A ∩ B) / P(A), where P(A) > 0

docs.title: Independence vs. Mutual Exclusivity
docs.text:
• Independent events CAN occur together
  • Mutually exclusive events CANNOT occur together
  • Two events cannot be both independent and mutually exclusive (unless one has probability 0)

docs.title: Addition Rule Selection
docs.text:
• Use basic addition rule ONLY for mutually exclusive events
  • Always use general addition rule when events can overlap

docs.title: Example: Card Drawing
docs.text: Drawing cards from a standard deck: - P(Drawing a King) = 4/52 = 1/13 - P(Drawing a Heart) = 13/52 = 1/4 - P(Drawing a King of Hearts) = 1/52 - P(Drawing a King OR a Heart) = 15/52 (using general addition rule)

docs.title: Example: Rolling Dice
docs.text: Rolling two dice: - P(Sum = 7) = 6/36 = 1/6 - P(First die = 6) = 1/6 - These events are independent: knowing the sum doesn't tell us the value of the first die

docs.title: Testing for Independence
docs.text: To verify if events A and B are independent, check if: P(A ∩ B) = P(A) × P(B)

docs.title: Testing for Mutual Exclusivity
docs.text: To verify if events A and B are mutually exclusive, check if: P(A ∩ B) = 0

docs.title: Law of Total Probability
docs.text: For a partition of events B₁, B₂, ..., Bₙ: P(A) = P(A|B₁)P(B₁) + P(A|B₂)P(B₂) + ... + P(A|Bₙ)P(Bₙ)

docs.title: Bayes' Theorem
docs.text: P(A|B) = P(B|A)P(A) / P(B)

docs.title: Practice Problems
docs.text:
1. If P(A) = 0.3, P(B) = 0.4, and P(A ∩ B) = 0.12, calculate:
  2. P(A ∪ B)

  3. Are A and B independent?

  4. Three cards are drawn from a deck without replacement. Calculate:

  5. P(All are aces)
  6. P(All are the same suit)

[Solutions provided in separate section]
